title: Correctness is not Faithfulness in Retrieval Augmented Generation Attributions
one_line_takeaway: Citation support is not enough for trustworthy RAG; a model may cite documents that support an answer even when it did not actually rely on them.
d4d_relevance: Highly relevant to a T3 semantic maritime COP because denied-network decision support must distinguish verified provenance from post-hoc citation decoration. The paper supports designing COP briefings where every vessel status, threat assessment, route recommendation, or anomaly explanation carries both source support and faithfulness evidence.
methods_or_architecture: conceptual separation between whether a citation supports a claim and whether the model genuinely used that citation
desiderata for trustworthy citations beyond accuracy
experimental evaluation of post-rationalization in RAG attributions
faithfulness assessment as an additional layer on top of citation correctness
outputs_metrics: reported finding that up to 57% of citations may be unfaithful
prototype_hooks: add separate COP indicators for claim support and attribution faithfulness
log retrieval context actually available at generation time for each semantic COP update
show provenance chains for AIS, radar, intelligence reports, weather, and human observations
flag briefings where citations appear supportive but were not part of the generation context
build an attribution audit mode for commander summaries and route recommendations
store compact source fingerprints for disconnected or bandwidth-limited maritime nodes
pitch_evidence: Citations alone can create false confidence in AI-generated operational summaries
For maritime COP over denied networks, provenance must survive degraded connectivity and later synchronization
The reported unfaithful-citation rate gives a concrete reason to invest in attribution auditing
Faithfulness metrics can be pitched as a safety layer for human-machine teaming in contested environments
limitations_risks: Only abstract text was available, so implementation details and datasets are unclear
The paper is about general RAG/IR rather than maritime command-and-control specifically
Faithfulness evaluation may depend heavily on access to retrieval logs or generation traces
Operational users may still over-trust cited outputs unless the UI clearly separates support from reliance
Additional attribution checks may add latency or complexity in bandwidth-constrained environments
